lessons.title: Why It Matters & Know Your Audience
lessons.content:
Imagine you solve a tricky problem brilliantly, but when you explain it, nobody understands a word. To them, it is as if you did nothing. That is the hard truth of **technical communication** (sharing technical ideas so other people actually get them): a great idea or result that others cannot understand is wasted. Being able to explain your work clearly is not an optional extra; it is a core skill, because clear communication is how good work actually has an impact.

The foundational rule is simple: **know your audience**. That means tailor your message to *who* is reading or listening and what they need. The same idea should be told differently to different people. Think about a car problem. To a **mechanic** you might say the timing belt is worn; to your **grandparent** you would just say the car needs a repair before a long trip. Same fact, different words, because the listeners are different.

A big part of this is **jargon** — special insider words that experts use as shorthand. Jargon is fine *between experts*, where it saves time. But for a non-expert it becomes a wall: they hear unfamiliar terms and get lost. So for a non-expert, either avoid the jargon or define it in plain words the first time you use it.

Here is a handy habit borrowed from the news: a newspaper puts the **headline before the article**, so you learn the main point in one line before any detail. People skim, decide if they care, then read on. Good communication works the same way.

To recap: communicating clearly is how your work reaches people; always start by asking who your audience is and what they need; and keep jargon for experts while using plain language for everyone else.

items.prompt: Why is communicating your work clearly described as a core skill, not an optional extra?
choices: Because a great idea or result that others cannot understand has little impact — it is wasted
Because long explanations always impress people more than short ones
Because only the smartest people are allowed to explain things
Because the work itself does not matter as long as you talk a lot
items.explanation: Clear communication is how good work actually reaches and helps people; a brilliant result nobody understands might as well not exist. Length does not equal impact, communicating is for everyone (not just the 'smartest'), and the work absolutely does matter — communication is what lets it count.

items.prompt: What does the foundational rule 'know your audience' actually mean?
choices: Always use the most technical language you can, to sound expert
Send the exact same message to everyone, no matter who they are
Tailor the message to who is reading or listening and what they need
Only ever communicate with other experts and ignore everyone else
items.explanation: Knowing your audience means shaping the detail, words, and tone to fit the specific reader or listener and their needs. Maximum jargon, one identical message for all, and talking only to experts all ignore who is actually on the receiving end — which is the whole point of the rule.

items.prompt: You need to describe the SAME car problem to a mechanic and to your grandparent. Which approach fits 'know your audience'?
choices: Give both of them the identical, fully technical description
Tell the mechanic the technical detail, and tell your grandparent the plain version they need
Refuse to explain it to your grandparent because they are not an expert
Use even more jargon with your grandparent so they learn the terms
items.explanation: Same fact, different audiences: the mechanic can handle the technical detail, while your grandparent needs the plain version ("it needs a repair before the trip"). One identical technical message loses the non-expert, refusing to explain is unhelpful, and piling on jargon makes it worse, not better.

items.prompt: What is 'jargon'?
choices: Any sentence that is longer than ten words
Special insider words that experts use as shorthand
A type of diagram used only in reports
Words that are always wrong and should never be used
items.explanation: Jargon is the specialist shorthand experts use among themselves; it saves time between people who share the background. It is not about sentence length and it is not a diagram. Jargon is not 'always wrong' — it is useful between experts — but it becomes a barrier for non-experts.

items.prompt: When is jargon appropriate, and when should you avoid or define it?
choices: Always use it everywhere; it makes you sound knowledgeable
Never use it with anyone, ever, under any circumstances
It is fine between experts, but for a non-expert you should avoid it or define it in plain words
Only use it with non-experts, never with fellow experts
items.explanation: Jargon is efficient shorthand between experts who share the meaning, but for a non-expert it is a wall — so avoid it or define it plainly on first use. Using it everywhere loses non-experts, banning it entirely throws away useful shorthand among experts, and the expert/non-expert roles in the last option are backwards.

items.prompt: A newspaper puts the headline before the article. What lesson does this offer for communicating?
choices: Hide the main point until the very end so people read everything
Give the main point first, in one line, before the detail
Never use any headings or summaries
Make every message exactly as long as a full news article
items.explanation: A headline delivers the main point up front so a skimming reader gets it immediately, then can choose to read on — good communication does the same. Hiding the point until the end forces readers to dig, dropping headings removes a useful signpost, and matching an article's length is irrelevant to the lesson.

items.prompt: You wrote a perfect report, but your manager reads it and says, 'I have no idea what this means.' What went wrong?
choices: Nothing — if the work is correct, it does not matter whether anyone understands it
The report should have been twice as long to be clearer
The communication failed: a correct result that the reader cannot understand has little impact
The manager simply is not smart enough to be worth explaining to
items.explanation: Even a correct result loses its value if the intended reader cannot follow it — the communication, not the work, fell short here. Correctness alone is not enough, more length would likely make it worse, and blaming the reader ignores your job to communicate for your audience.

items.prompt: You are explaining a software feature to a non-technical customer. Which version best fits this audience?
choices: "It writes to a buffer, flushes the cache, then commits the transaction atomically."
"Your changes are saved instantly, so you will not lose your work."
"The asynchronous write pipeline guarantees durability via a two-phase commit."
Read them the full internal technical design document, word for word
items.explanation: A non-technical customer needs the plain meaning — their work is saved and safe — with the jargon stripped out. The buffer/cache/transaction wording and the two-phase-commit wording are expert shorthand that would lose them, and reading internal technical docs aloud is exactly the wrong level of detail for this audience.

items.prompt: An expert teammate, a busy manager, and a non-technical customer ask about the same result. What does 'know your audience' suggest?
choices: Give all three the identical message so it is fair
Give each the level of detail and language they need — more for the expert, plain words for the customer
Give the most detail to the customer and the least to the expert
Only answer the expert, since the others would not understand anyway
items.explanation: Different audiences need different detail and wording: the expert can take the full technical version, the manager wants the short result, and the customer needs plain language. One identical message ignores their needs, giving the customer the most technical detail is backwards, and answering only the expert abandons the people you most need to reach.

lessons.title: Lead With the Point, Structure & Be Precise
lessons.content:
Now that you tailor a message to your audience, the next question is *what order* to say things in. Busy people do not want a slow build-up to a surprise ending. They want the conclusion first. This is **BLUF — Bottom Line Up Front**: state your takeaway, your ask, or your recommendation right at the start, then give the supporting details.

Everyday version: telling someone 'the meeting moved to 3pm' *before* the backstory of why. The bottom line lands first; the explanation follows for anyone who wants it. The opposite — burying the conclusion at the very end after pages of build-up — forces a busy reader to dig for the one thing they needed. Do not make them dig.

Next comes **structure and clarity**, which simply means organising your message so it is easy to follow and easy to skim. A good order is overview, then details, then conclusion. Use short sentences and put one idea per paragraph. Add **headings** (mini-titles), **lists** (bullet points), and **white space** (blank gaps) so a reader can scan and find what they need. And cut needless words — if a word adds nothing, delete it. Think of a recipe with clear numbered steps versus the same recipe written as one dense block of text; the steps are far easier to use.

Finally, **be precise**, which means using concrete numbers and specifics instead of vague claims. 'It is much faster' tells the reader almost nothing. 'It loads in 1 second instead of 4' tells them exactly what changed. Specifics build trust and remove guesswork; vague words leave the reader unsure.

To recap: lead with the bottom line, structure your message so it is skimmable with headings and lists, cut the fluff, and replace vague claims with concrete specifics.

items.prompt: What does BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) mean?
choices: Save the most important point for the very last line
State your conclusion, ask, or recommendation first, then give the details
Never include any details, only the conclusion
Put the longest section at the front of the document
items.explanation: BLUF means leading with the takeaway, ask, or recommendation, then supporting it with detail — the busy reader gets the point immediately. Saving it for last is the buried-conclusion mistake BLUF avoids, you still include the details (just after the point), and it is about order of importance, not length.

items.prompt: Why is leading with the point (BLUF) helpful for a busy reader?
choices: It makes the message longer so there is more to read
It gives them the conclusion or ask first, so they get what they need without digging
It hides the recommendation so they must read every word
It removes the need to ever explain anything
items.explanation: Busy people want the answer up front; BLUF hands them the conclusion or ask immediately, and the detail is there if they want it. It is not about adding length, the whole idea is to NOT hide the recommendation, and the explanation still follows — it just comes after the point.

items.prompt: Which email opening best follows 'lead with the point'?
choices: A long paragraph of background, with the actual request hidden in the final sentence
"Please approve the budget by Friday. Details and reasoning are below."
Several pages of history before mentioning what you need
An opening that never states the request at all
items.explanation: Stating the ask and deadline first, then offering details, is BLUF done right — the reader knows immediately what is needed. Hiding the request in the last sentence or after pages of history buries the point, and never stating the request leaves the reader with nothing to act on.

items.prompt: What does it mean to give a message good 'structure and clarity'?
choices: Write one long unbroken block of text with no breaks
Organise it logically and make it skimmable: short sentences, one idea per paragraph, headings, lists, and white space
Use as many long, complex sentences as possible
Scatter the ideas randomly so the reader stays alert
items.explanation: Structure and clarity mean a logical order plus skimmable formatting — short sentences, one idea per paragraph, headings, lists, and white space — so readers find what they need fast. A single dense block, deliberately complex sentences, and random ordering all make the message harder, not easier, to follow.

items.prompt: A recipe written as clear numbered steps is easier to use than the same recipe as one dense block of text. Why?
choices: Because numbered steps add structure and white space, so the reader can follow and find each part
Because the dense block always contains better cooking advice
Because numbered steps remove all the actual instructions
Because a wall of text is always quicker to read than a list
items.explanation: Numbered steps give structure and white space, letting the eye land on one action at a time — that is what makes them easy to follow. The dense block is not better content, the steps keep all the instructions (just organised), and a wall of text is harder, not quicker, to use.

items.prompt: What does 'cut needless words' improve about your writing?
choices: It makes the message harder to understand on purpose
It removes meaning so the reader knows less
It makes the message clearer and quicker to read by dropping words that add nothing
It is only about making the document fit on one page
items.explanation: Removing words that carry no meaning leaves the real point standing out, so the reader gets it faster. Cutting fluff does not harm understanding — it helps it — you keep the meaning while dropping the filler, and the goal is clarity, not merely shrinking page count.

items.prompt: Which statement is precise and concrete rather than a vague claim?
choices: "It is much faster now."
"Performance has improved significantly."
"It loads in 1 second instead of 4."
"Things are better than before."
items.explanation: "Loads in 1 second instead of 4" gives concrete numbers, so the reader knows exactly what changed. "Much faster", "improved significantly", and "better than before" are all vague — they sound positive but tell the reader almost nothing measurable.

items.prompt: Why are concrete specifics usually better than vague claims in technical writing?
choices: Vague claims are always more honest than numbers
Specifics tell the reader exactly what happened and build trust, while vague words leave them guessing
Numbers should be avoided because they confuse every reader
Vague claims are better because they can never be proven wrong
items.explanation: Concrete specifics ('1 second instead of 4') remove guesswork and let the reader judge the claim, which builds trust. Vagueness is not more honest, well-chosen numbers clarify rather than confuse, and 'can never be proven wrong' is a weakness of vague claims, not a virtue.

items.prompt: Pulling the writing tips together, which message is best built?
choices: Bottom line first, organised with headings and short paragraphs, and precise specifics instead of vague claims
The same wording sent to every audience, with the conclusion buried at the end
One dense block of text with no headings and only vague claims
Maximum jargon, no structure, and the main point left out entirely
items.explanation: Good writing leads with the point, is structured and skimmable, and uses concrete specifics — exactly the first option. One identical message with a buried conclusion, a dense block of vague claims, and jargon-heavy text with no point are all the habits this lesson warns against.

lessons.title: Visuals & Presenting
lessons.content:
Words are not always the best tool. Often a good **visual** — a picture, chart, diagram, table, or flowchart — makes a point faster than paragraphs ever could. People say 'a picture is worth a thousand words', and a clear map beats written directions. The skill is picking the *right* visual for the job.

Think of four common choices. A **chart** (like a line going up over time) is best for a **trend** or a comparison. A **table** (rows and columns of values) is best when you need **exact numbers**. A **diagram** (boxes joined by arrows) is best for showing **how parts connect**. A **flowchart** (steps with arrows) is best for a **process**, the order of steps. Once you pick, keep it simple and uncluttered: **label** the axes or parts, and make sure the visual makes **one** clear point. A cluttered do-everything diagram that tries to show all things at once just confuses people.

**Presenting** is talking through your idea to a group, usually with **slides** (the screens behind you). The golden rule: slides **support you**; they are not your script. So use few words, big visuals, and one idea per slide. Never just read dense paragraphs off the screen — if every word is on the slide, the audience does not need you. Know your single key message, and **practise** beforehand so it flows.

One more thing: handle questions honestly. If you are asked something you do not know, saying 'I do not know, I will find out' builds more trust than bluffing a wrong answer.

To wrap up the whole sub-topic: communicate for your audience, put the bottom line first, structure your message so it is skimmable, use the right visual to make one clear point, keep slides simple, and be precise and honest. That is how good work actually has impact.

items.prompt: Why is a good visual (chart, diagram, table, or flowchart) often better than paragraphs of text?
choices: Because visuals can make a point faster and clearer, like a map beating written directions
Because visuals never need any labels or explanation
Because text should always be avoided completely
Because a picture always proves your claim is correct
items.explanation: A well-chosen visual can convey a point at a glance — a map versus written directions is the classic example. But visuals still need labels, text is not banned (the two work together), and a picture conveys a point, it does not by itself prove the claim is true.

items.prompt: You want to show how a value has risen over the last twelve months — a trend over time. Which visual fits best?
choices: A table of raw numbers with no shape
A chart, such as a line going up over time
A flowchart of steps
A cluttered diagram showing every part of the whole system
items.explanation: A chart (a line over time) is built to show a trend; the rising shape makes the change obvious at a glance. A table hides the shape in raw numbers, a flowchart is for a process not a trend, and a do-everything cluttered diagram is the opposite of making one clear point.

items.prompt: You need the reader to see the EXACT values for ten items, not a general shape. Which visual fits best?
choices: A line chart, which shows shape but not precise figures
A flowchart of the process
A table, with rows and columns of the exact numbers
A single photo of the items
items.explanation: A table lays out exact values in rows and columns, which is exactly what you want when precise figures matter. A line chart shows the trend but not easy-to-read exact numbers, a flowchart is for steps in a process, and a photo shows none of the values.

items.prompt: You want to show the ORDER of steps in a process, from start to finish. Which visual fits best?
choices: A flowchart, with steps connected by arrows in order
A table of exact values
A line chart of a trend
A diagram with no arrows showing only loose boxes
items.explanation: A flowchart links steps with arrows to show the order of a process, which is precisely this job. A table is for exact values, a line chart is for trends, and a set of loose boxes with no arrows fails to show any sequence or direction.

items.prompt: Beyond picking the right type, what makes a visual GOOD?
choices: Cramming in as much information as possible so it covers everything at once
Keeping it simple and uncluttered, labelling the parts, and making one clear point
Leaving the axes and parts unlabelled so it looks cleaner
Using a do-everything diagram that mixes trends, values, and steps together
items.explanation: A good visual is simple and uncluttered, has labelled axes or parts, and makes a single clear point. Cramming in everything and a do-everything diagram overwhelm the reader, and dropping labels does not make it cleaner — it makes it impossible to interpret.

items.prompt: What is the right relationship between you and your slides when presenting?
choices: The slides are your full script, to be read aloud word for word
The slides support you — few words, big visuals, one idea per slide — while you do the explaining
The slides should contain every detail so the audience does not need you
The more dense text on each slide, the more professional it looks
items.explanation: Slides are a visual aid that supports the speaker: few words, big visuals, one idea each, while you provide the explanation. Treating slides as a script to read aloud, putting every detail on them, or packing them with dense text all make you redundant and lose the audience.

items.prompt: Which slide is better designed for a talk?
choices: A slide with one headline and a single big, clear chart
A slide filled with a dense paragraph that the speaker reads aloud
A slide listing every fact about the project in tiny text
A slide with several unrelated ideas crammed together
items.explanation: One headline plus a single big visual is the good-slide pattern: one idea, few words, easy to grasp while you talk. A dense paragraph read aloud, tiny all-the-facts text, and several crammed-in ideas all violate 'one idea per slide, few words' and lose the room.

items.prompt: During the questions after your talk, someone asks something you genuinely do not know. What is the best response?
choices: Make up a confident-sounding answer so you do not look unsure
Say honestly, "I do not know, but I will find out," and follow up
Refuse to answer any questions at all
Change the subject and hope nobody notices
items.explanation: Honesty about what you do not know, plus a promise to find out, builds trust and protects your credibility. Bluffing a confident wrong answer can mislead people and backfire, refusing all questions looks evasive, and dodging the subject is just a softer form of bluffing.

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up clear technical communication overall?
choices: Send the same message to everyone, bury the bottom line, use a cluttered do-everything diagram, and read dense slides aloud
Communicate for your audience, put the bottom line first, structure for skimming, use the right visual to make one clear point, keep slides simple, and be precise and honest
Use as much jargon as possible and never use any visuals
Focus only on the work itself; how you explain it does not matter
items.explanation: The second option is the whole sub-topic in one line: audience-first, bottom line up front, skimmable structure, the right visual making one point, simple slides, and precision plus honesty. The other choices gather the exact mistakes — one-size messages, buried points, cluttered visuals, dense read-aloud slides, jargon, and ignoring communication — that defeat impact.
